X6.1.label: Question X6.1
X6.1.topic: Entering the PMI market: Risks, investigations, and responses.
X6.1.synopsis: Risks of entering the individual PMI market, investigations required into poor early experience (high claims and initial expenses), and possible responses to these problems such as revising underwriting, raising premiums, withdrawing, or review of reinsurance.
X6.1.scenario: A large mutual life insurance company is considering diversifying into the individual private medical insurance (PMI) market.

(i).question: Discuss the risks to the company of such a development.

Risks to the company: The company has no expertise in this area (eg in underwriting or claims handling). [½]
The market could be very competitive ... [½]
... resulting in lower than expected new business volumes ... [½]
... or the need to cut premiums. [½]
The company will therefore experience higher relative expenses (eg to cover fixed overheads) ... [½]
... which will reduce profitability. [½]
The new business may not be sufficient to cover the initial setup costs ... [½]
... (eg the cost of developing brand new administration and IT systems to process the very high volume of low value claims). [1]
The new product may cannibalise other product sales (thereby losing the company money due to the development, advertising and initial expenses) ... [1]
... or may damage the existing reputation of the company (since PMI is a product that experiences a high volume of complaints). [1]
The lack of data may mean that the premium structure or level is incorrect. [½]
The risk is therefore that the product is a loss-leader. [½]
Poor claims experience relative to that expected due to:
anti-selection, [½]
moral hazard (overuse by patients or doctors), [½]
medical inflation higher than expected, [½]
new expensive treatments, [½]
state provision reducing. [½]
Poor persistency. [½]
Lack of suitable reinsurance. [½]
Distribution channels may not be appropriate. [½]
Capital requirements may be significant, tying up free assets. [½]
Solvency II / regulatory changes could alter profitability. [½]

(ii).question: Three years after launching the product the level of profits are lower than expected. State the investigations you would carry out to determine the causes of this.

Investigations into the problems: Need to establish if the problem is poor sales, high expenses or high claims. [1]
Split data by distribution channel, age, gender, occupation, etc to find the source of the problem. [1]
Compare actual experience with pricing assumptions for each component. [1]
Check claim administration and underwriting standards to see if they are being applied correctly. [1]
Analyse expenses to separate out fixed and variable components and initial vs renewal. [1]
Analyse withdrawal rates and whether selective withdrawals are occurring. [1]
Check competitors' premiums, product designs and market share. [1]
Review reinsurance arrangements and recovery levels. [1]
Obtain feedback from the salesforce and customer complaints. [1]
Investigate any tax changes affecting PMI. [½]
Investigate any economic changes (eg downturn reducing demand). [½]
Consult with medical experts regarding new treatments or medical inflation trends. [1]
Perform sensitivity testing on key parameters using a model office to assess total financial impact. [1]

(iii).question: Discuss the possible responses the company could make following your investigations.

Possible responses: Revise underwriting procedures to be stricter, or ask different medical questions. [1]
Implement better expense control (reduce overheads, or outsource administration). [1]
Redesign the product by adjusting benefits, imposing limits or increasing the excess. [1]
Change the premiums charged for new business and at renewal. [1]
Care is needed to avoid a cycle of selective withdrawals by healthier lives. [1]
Review reinsurance (eg switch from quota share to non-proportional or seek better rates). [1]
Improve claims control (eg use specific hospital networks, pre-authorisation of treatments). [1]
Improve marketing or change the target market to attract a better risk profile. [1]
Provide better training to the salesforce and claims handlers. [1]
Withdraw from the market entirely and place the block in run-off. [1]
Sell the block of business to a competitor. [1]
Accept as a loss leader if it generates profitable cross-sales for other products. [1]

X6.2.label: Question X6.2
X6.2.topic: Reasons for poor performance, improvements, and strategic actions for a PMI subsidiary.
X6.2.synopsis: Analysis of low volumes, low profitability, and higher technical provisions. Evaluation of marketing suggestions (loyalty and claims-related discounts, internet sales). Strategic actions related to competition, product design, persistency, and overall business management.
X6.2.scenario: A large general insurer has a private medical insurance (PMI) subsidiary. The subsidiary has recently experienced poor performance: low volumes of new business, poor persistency, low profitability, and higher than expected technical provisions. The marketing department has suggested a loyalty discount and selling through the internet to improve performance.

(i) (a).question: Suggest reasons for the low volumes of new business and poor persistency.

Low volumes of new business: Competition: The target market is highly competitive. [½]
Competitors may be selling similar products more cheaply. [½]
They may accept smaller profits initially to build market share ... [½]
... taking advantage of the initial selection effect. [½]
Competitors may use different rating factors (eg target young people) ... [½]
Competitors may have inaccurate premiums (will be corrected over time). [½]
They may use stricter underwriting to attract better risks at a cheaper premium. [½]
They may have more efficient technology or lower administration costs. [½]
They may offer a more attractive product or innovative marketing. [½]
The sales method used by competitors may be very successful. [½]
Poor persistency: The main deterrent to renewing is the size of premium increases on renewal. [½]
Overall annual increase is often in excess of price inflation. [½]
Premiums keep pace with increasing claims ... [½]
... and each individual's premium increases to allow for increasing age. [½]
Non-claimers may not wish to renew because they feel they have had no benefit. [½]
Policyholders may leave due to poor customer service or bad publicity. [½]
General less demand for PMI (eg due to improvements in State healthcare). [½]
Economic downturn resulting in less disposable income. [½]

(i) (b).question: Suggest reasons for the low profitability.

Claims experience: The falling profits could be due to rising claim rates. [½]
Anti-selection: mix of business may be skewed towards sectors with greater claim increases. [1]
Average age of policyholders may be increasing. [½]
Number of claims per policy could be increasing due to:
genuine increase in people requiring treatment, [½]
increasing awareness of PMI cover benefits, [½]
initial selection effect wearing off (increasing maturity). [½]
Amount of claims could be increasing due to:
increasing medical inflation (higher than general inflation), [½]
increasing costs of medical equipment or consultants' fees, [½]
impact of improving medical technology / new expensive treatments. [½]
Standards of medical underwriting could be poor (insufficient evidence, poor training, cost cutting). [½]
Poor claims control (treatments covered that shouldn't be). [½]
Premiums and Expenses: Premium rates may be lower than required due to competitive pressure. [1]
Overall premium levels may be less than assumed due to changes in mix of business ... [½]
... eg people purchasing products with higher excesses or reducing family sizes. [½]
Premium rates may be insufficient to cover technical provisions. [½]
Expenses may have increased by more than expected (eg administration costs). [½]

(i) (c).question: Suggest reasons for the higher than expected technical provisions.

Technical provisions (TP = BEL + RM): Increase in best estimate liability (BEL) or risk margin (RM). [½]
BEL may have increased due to a change to model points used to calculate the approximate BEL. [½]
BEL may have increased due to a change in assumptions (should always be best estimate). [½]
BEL may have increased due to a lower discount rate used. [½]
Assumptions could need updating due to claims becoming longer tailed. [½]
Assumptions could need updating due to a backlog in payment of claims. [½]
RM may have increased due to a change in capital required for each future year. [½]
RM may have increased due to a higher cost of capital rate. [½]
RM may have increased due to a lower discount rate used. [½]
RM may have increased due to a change in method for approximating RM. [½]
Calculated provisions may have been incorrect due to an inappropriate method or incorrect data. [1]

(ii).question: Discuss the marketing department's suggested improvements: offering a loyalty discount to personal customers, and selling through the internet.

Loyalty discount to personal customers: Discount size reflects the time the customer has held the policy. [½]
Advantages: encourages retention / keeps in-force volumes high. [½]
Justified as cost of acquiring new business is greater than retaining at renewal. [½]
Attracts new customers who wouldn't otherwise purchase. [½]
Disadvantages: per-policy profits from older customers will be lower (cross-subsidising). [½]
Claims are expected to increase as initial selection wears off. [½]
Discount impact may be absorbed by inflationary premium increases, unappreciated by customers. [½]
Expected effect must be allowed for in initial premium Pricing. [½]
Alternatively, a claims-related discount (NCD) based on claim-free years could be applied. [½]
Selling through the internet: Advantages: new distribution channel may increase sales. [½]
May appeal to a different market segment (eg younger, healthier customers). [½]
Quicker sales, reducing selling costs (less human involvement). [½]
If established internet operations exist, can be adapted quickly and cheaply. [½]
Disadvantages: less information collected, making accurate medical underwriting difficult. [1]
Moratorium method of underwriting more likely. [½]
Greater risk of customer misunderstanding (exclusions, limits, moratorium). [½]
Simpler product design needed to reduce complaint risk. [½]
Greater risk of anti-selection or customers lying for cheaper price. [½]
Insurer must ensure rating structures are robust and check validity of information. [½]
Set-up costs of establishing internet operation need allowance. [½]
Financial modelling should be carried out to assess the financial impact. [½]

(iii).question: Suggest other actions the subsidiary could take to improve performance.

Competition and Product design: Investigate reasons competitors are able to sell cheaply. [½]
Implement new processes quickly if competitors' approaches are sustainable. [½]
Reduce benefits to offer more affordable products (eg exclude certain conditions/treatments). [1]
Restrict treatments to certain hospitals or negotiate special rates. [½]
Limit payments per treatment, restrict treatments per year, or limit aggregate payments. [1]
Provide private cover only if State waiting lists are long. [½]
Impose or increase an excess/deductible per claim. [½]
Drawback: restricting benefits may be unpopular. [½]
Exclusions should be chosen carefully, eg treatments available from the State. [½]
Benefit restrictions must be communicated carefully. [½]
Encourage use of State treatments by offering generous cash payments if treated by the State. [½]
Offer cash rather than indemnity benefits (cheaper and attractive to certain segments). [1]
Persistency and Other actions: Encourage payment by direct debit to improve persistency. [½]
Establish a special team to contact customers before renewal to resolve grievances. [1]
Implement pricing structures that limit rate of price increases (eg guarantee max 5% pa). [½]
Guaranteeing premiums significantly increases risk to the insurer. [½]
Maintain good quality customer service to aid retention. [½]
Include cheap benefits (eg health information lines) to give perceived value for non-claimers. [1]
Undertake analysis of profitability by product/source and reduce unprofitable business. [1]
Review selling methods, investigate new distribution channels. [½]
Review claims handling and underwriting standards to ensure effectiveness. [½]
Review administration expenses to balance efficiency with customer service. [½]
Analyse/rectify reasons for technical provision increases (errors/processing). [½]
Amend business plans for expected changes in government or regulation. [½]
Review investment strategy (eg move to closer matched position or cash). [½]
Regularly monitor effects of actions and adjust if necessary. [½]
